text.title: Quality Management — Framework and Three Processes
text.summary: The Plan-Manage-Control quality triad, quality concepts, metrics taxonomy, and the machine-readable DoD pattern.

rule.statement: Build quality in through prevention; do not test it in through detection — prevention costs 10-100x less than production defect resolution.
rule.rationale: Deming's empirical finding from manufacturing: defects found at code review cost a fraction of defects found in production. Every skipped review is a deferred cost with interest.

list.item: Quality = degree to which requirements are met (not "high quality" — that conflates grade with conformance).
Grade = category of features (premium vs. basic); low grade is a business decision, not a defect.
Prevention = building quality in (code review, pair programming, TDD).
Detection = finding defects after they exist (testing, inspections, UAT) — necessary but more expensive than prevention.
Plan Quality (before work begins): define standards, metrics, and targets. Lock in quality/dod.yaml per repo.
Manage Quality (assurance, during work): ensure processes produce quality — code reviews, DoD enforcement, automated testing requirements, retrospectives.
Control Quality (verification, at completion): verify deliverables meet standards — testing, inspections, defect tracking.
Product metrics: performance, reliability, usability, defect escape rate.
Process metrics: code coverage %, code review completion rate, DoD compliance %, defect density.
Perception metrics: customer satisfaction (NPS, CSAT), uptime SLA, response time SLO.

rule.statement: Track defect escape rate (bugs found in production / total bugs) as a leading indicator; apply SPC charting to distinguish signal from noise.
rule.rationale: A single defect count is a lagging snapshot; escape rate trend exposes whether the quality system is improving or degrading before customer impact accumulates.

section.title: Definition of Done as Code

rule.statement: Lock DoD per repo in quality/dod.yaml and version it — agents and CI read the file, not human prose.
rule.rationale: Human-prose DoD drift within weeks of authoring; a machine-readable file enforced by CI makes "done" unambiguous and diff-auditable when the team updates standards.

rule.statement: Separate must items from should items in DoD; only must items block merge — should items open a follow-up issue automatically.
rule.rationale: A DoD that blocks on every item becomes a bypass incentive under delivery pressure; the must/should split preserves coverage while maintaining merge velocity.

section.reference: PR gate script enforcing the DoD
